name: hpa
context: Auto-scale Kubernetes workloads with HorizontalPodAutoscaler — scale Pods based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics
info: The HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) automatically scales the number of Pod replicas in a [[kubernetes/deployments]] based on observed metrics. HPA watches CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom/external metrics and adjusts replicas to meet target thresholds. Agents use HPA to ensure applications scale under load and scale down during quiet periods to save resources. This skill covers HPA manifests, metric types (resource, custom, external), scaling behavior, stabilization windows, and the relationship with the metrics-server.
body:
# HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA)

HPA automatically adjusts the number of Pod replicas based on metrics. More traffic → more Pods. Less traffic → fewer Pods.

## Prerequisites

HPA requires a metrics source. Install metrics-server for CPU/memory metrics:

```bash
# Install metrics-server (required for CPU/memory HPA)
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

# Verify it's running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=metrics-server

# Test: check node/pod metrics
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods -n production
```

For custom metrics, install Prometheus Adapter or KEDA.

## Basic HPA (CPU-based)

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-app-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-app
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70    # scale up when avg CPU > 70%
```

## CPU + Memory HPA

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-app-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-app
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 80
```

When multiple metrics are specified, HPA calculates the desired replica count for each metric and uses the **highest** value.

## Scaling Behavior

Control how fast HPA scales up and down:

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-app-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-app
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 50
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60    # wait 60s before scaling up again
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 100                     # can double replica count
          periodSeconds: 60
        - type: Pods
          value: 5                       # or add up to 5 pods
          periodSeconds: 60
      selectPolicy: Max                  # use whichever allows more scaling
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300   # wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 10                      # remove max 10% of replicas
          periodSeconds: 60
      selectPolicy: Min                  # scale down conservatively
```

## kubectl Commands

```bash
# Create HPA imperatively
kubectl autoscale deployment web-app \
  --min=3 --max=20 --cpu-percent=70 \
  -n production

# Create from manifest
kubectl apply -f hpa.yaml

# List HPAs
kubectl get hpa -n production

# Describe (shows current metrics, desired replicas, events)
kubectl describe hpa web-app-hpa -n production

# Watch HPA decisions in real-time
kubectl get hpa -n production -w

# Check current pod metrics
kubectl top pods -n production -l app=web-app

# Delete HPA
kubectl delete hpa web-app-hpa -n production
```

## How HPA Calculates Replicas

```
desiredReplicas = ceil(currentReplicas × (currentMetricValue / targetMetricValue))
```

Example: 5 replicas at 90% CPU with target 70%:
```
desiredReplicas = ceil(5 × (90 / 70)) = ceil(6.43) = 7
```

HPA checks metrics every 15 seconds (configurable via `--horizontal-pod-autoscaler-sync-period`).

## Custom Metrics

Scale based on application-specific metrics (requires Prometheus Adapter or similar):

```yaml
metrics:
  - type: Pods
    pods:
      metric:
        name: http_requests_per_second
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: 1000    # scale when avg RPS > 1000 per pod
  - type: Object
    object:
      describedObject:
        apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
        kind: Ingress
        name: web-app
      metric:
        name: requests_per_second
      target:
        type: Value
        value: 5000
```

## Important Notes

- **Pods MUST have resource requests** defined for CPU/memory HPA to work. Without requests, HPA cannot calculate utilization percentages.
- HPA and manual `kubectl scale` conflict — HPA will override manual scaling.
- HPA scales [[kubernetes/deployments]], StatefulSets, and ReplicaSets.
- For event-driven autoscaling (queue depth, etc.), consider KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaler).
- For vertical scaling (resize CPU/memory), use Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) — but do not use VPA and HPA on the same metric.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace |

sections.title: CPU and memory metrics
sections.body: HPA scales on `type: Resource` metrics (cpu, memory) from metrics-server. Target types: `Utilization` (percentage of request, e.g., 70%), `AverageValue` (absolute value per pod, e.g., 200m CPU). Pods MUST have resource requests set for Utilization to work. Install metrics-server: `kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml`. Verify with `kubectl top pods`.

sections.title: Scaling behavior and stabilization
sections.body: Control scaling speed with `spec.behavior`. `stabilizationWindowSeconds` prevents flapping — HPA considers the highest (scale-up) or lowest (scale-down) recommendation in the window. Policies define max change per period: `type: Percent` (e.g., double replicas), `type: Pods` (e.g., add 5 pods). `selectPolicy: Max` picks the most aggressive policy, `Min` picks the most conservative. Default: scale up immediately, scale down after 5 min stabilization.

sections.title: Custom and external metrics
sections.body: Scale on application metrics (RPS, queue depth, latency) via custom metrics API. Requires Prometheus Adapter, Datadog Cluster Agent, or similar. Metric types: `Pods` (per-pod average), `Object` (from a specific Kubernetes object), `External` (from outside the cluster, e.g., cloud queue depth). KEDA provides event-driven autoscaling with 60+ scalers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS, etc.).

links.context: Pods must have resource requests for HPA to work

related.note: HPA targets Deployments (or StatefulSets) for scaling
Pods must have resource requests defined for metric-based scaling

scripts.name: Create HPA
scripts.description: Create a HorizontalPodAutoscaler for a Deployment

scripts.name: HPA Status
scripts.description: Show current HPA status, metrics, and scaling decisions
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}

echo "=== HPAs in namespace $NS ==="
kubectl get hpa -n "$NS" -o wide
echo ""

for HPA in $(kubectl get hpa -n "$NS" -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do
  echo "=== $HPA ==="
  kubectl describe hpa "$HPA" -n "$NS" | grep -A 5 'Metrics:\|Min replicas:\|Conditions:'
  echo ""
done

echo "=== Pod Resource Usage ==="
kubectl top pods -n "$NS" 2>/dev/null || echo "metrics-server not available"

depends.reason: HPA targets Deployments for replica scaling
Pods must have resource requests for metric-based autoscaling
